perturbation.original_text: Terms of payment by the Agent to the Company for Products shipped to the Customer will be net thirty (30) days from the date of statement unless the Agent chooses to pay the Company via electronic funds transfer in which case the terms of payment will be net forty-five (45) days.
perturbation.changed_text: Terms of payment by the Agent to the Company for Products shipped to the Customer will be net ninety (90) days from the date of statement unless the Agent chooses to pay the Company via electronic funds transfer in which case the terms of payment will be net one hundred and twenty (120) days.
perturbation.explanation: This change extends the payment terms, which could violate the Massachusetts Prompt Payment Law if the Agent is a small business and the Customer is a larger entity, or if the goods are perishable agricultural products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E, M.G.L. c. 149, § 29F, and the Perishable Agricultural Commodities Act (PACA), 7 U.S. Code § 499b(4).
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E mandates prompt payment to subcontractors on public works projects, and M.G.L. c. 149, § 29F applies similar principles to private construction projects. These laws generally require payment within a shorter timeframe than 90-120 days. The Perishable Agricultural Commodities Act (PACA) requires prompt payment (often within 10 days) for perishable agricultural products, and extending payment terms to 90-120 days would violate PACA if the Products were considered perishable agricultural commodities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 29E Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 29E Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 29E: Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E. (a) As used in this section the following words shall have the following meanings, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: ''Contract for construction'', a contract for which a lien may be established under sections 2 or 4 of chapter 254 on a project for which the person whose contract with the project owner has an original contract price of $3,000,000 or more; provided, however, this shall not include projects containing or designed to contain at least 1 but not more than 4 dwelling units. ''Insolvent'', insolvent as defined under federal bankruptcy law or being a debtor in a proceeding commenced under federal bankruptcy law or under the corresponding law of another country, or being a debtor in a receivership proceeding or having made an assignment for the benefit of creditors. (b) A communication required in this section to be in writing may be submitted in electronic form and by electronic means. (c) Every contract for construction shall provide reasonable time periods within which: (i) a person seeking payment under the contract shall submit written applications for periodic progress payments; (ii) the person receiving the application

(6) For any commission merchant, dealer, or broker, for a fraudulent purpose, to remove, alter, or tamper with any card, stencil, stamp, tag, or other notice placed upon any container or railroad car containing any perishable agricultural commodity, if such card, stencil, stamp, tag, or other notice contains a certificate or statement under authority of any Federal or State inspector or in compliance with any Federal or State law or regulation as to the grade or quality of the commodity contained in such container or railroad car or the State or country in which such commodity was produced. perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms to 90-120 days. The Law Explanation states this could violate M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E and § 29F, which mandate prompt payment to subcontractors. It also mentions PACA, which requires prompt payment for perishable agricultural products, often within 10 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses Section 29E, construction contracts and reasonable time periods. Extending the payment terms as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation introduces an inconsistency with potential prompt payment requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any claim for shortage in Product delivery must be made within ten (10) business days after the Customer's receipt of shipment.
perturbation.changed_text: Any claim for shortage in Product delivery must be made within two (2) business days after the Customer's receipt of shipment.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the claim period to 2 business days may be deemed unreasonable and could conflict with the implied warranty of merchantability under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) as adopted in Massachusetts, particularly if a reasonable inspection would take longer than two days. This also has to comply with consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 106 § 2-607 requires the buyer to notify the seller of any breach within a reasonable time after discovery. Two business days may not be a 'reasonable time' depending on the nature of the product and the complexity of the inspection. M.G.L. c. 93A prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce, and imposing an unreasonably short claim period could be considered a violation of this law if it effectively prevents customers from asserting legitimate claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-607 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-607 Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach after Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over Section 2-607 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-607: Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach after Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over Section 2–607. (1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted. (2) Acceptance of goods by the buyer precludes rejection of the goods accepted and if made with knowledge of a non-conformity cannot be revoked because of it unless the acceptance was on the reasonable assumption that the non-conformity would be seasonably cured but acceptance does not of itself impair any other remedy provided by this Article for non-conformity. (3) Where a tender has been accepted (a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach notify the seller of breach or be barred from any remedy; and (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of section 2–312) and the buyer is sued as a result of such a breach he must so notify the seller within a reasonable time after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from any remedy over for liability established by the litigation. (4) The burden is on the buyer to establish any breach with respect to the goods accepted. (5) Where the buyer is sued for breach of a warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over (a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time to file a claim from ten to two business days. M.G.L. c. 106 § 2-607, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that the buyer must notify the seller of a breach within a 'reasonable time'. Two business days may not be a reasonable time, depending on the product, potentially contradicting the law. Also, M.G.L. c. 93A, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, declares unfair or deceptive acts or practices unlawful; an unreasonably short claim period could be considered such a practice, especially if it prevents customers from asserting legitimate claims. This creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party fails in any material respect to observe or perform any of its obligations under this Agreement (with respect to the Agent, including but not limited to Agent performance criteria set forth in each Business Plan), which failure is not remedied within thirty (30) days (or, in the case of payments due, within five (5) business days), after receipt of written notice from the other party specifying such failure, this Agreement shall automatically terminate.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party fails in any material respect to observe or perform any of its obligations under this Agreement (with respect to the Agent, including but not limited to Agent performance criteria set forth in each Business Plan), which failure is not remedied within ten (10) days (or, in the case of payments due, within two (2) business days), after receipt of written notice from the other party specifying such failure, this Agreement shall automatically terminate.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the cure period to 10 days (or 2 days for payments) may be deemed unreasonable and could be considered a breach of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the failure to perform is due to circumstances beyond the party's control. This could violate Massachusetts law requiring good faith in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: M.G.L. c. 93A and Massachusetts common law regarding the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. A court could find that providing an unreasonably short cure period, particularly where significant performance is at stake, constitutes an unfair business practice. Massachusetts common law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, requiring parties to act honestly and in good faith. A short cure period might violate this covenant if it's designed to create a pretext for termination rather than a genuine opportunity to remedy the breach.

(b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the cure period for breaches, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law, as explained by the Law Explanation. M.G.L. c. 93A, Section 2(a), declares unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices unlawful, which could include setting an unreasonably short cure period. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that a shortened cure period may not provide a genuine opportunity to remedy a breach. This creates an inconsistency that contradicts the intent of fair dealing.
